3: Societies are looking for solutions to produce renewable energy in a sustainable way. In line with this, the European Union (EU) has set the goal to reach 20% of total energy consumption based on renewable resources by 2020 (European Union, 2009). Manure is one of the available resources with great potential for energy pro- duction through anaerobic digestion, and doing so triggers

significant reduction in the environmental impacts of manure management (Agostini et al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014, 2011). In the EU, manure-biogas production is currently far below its full potential (Birkmose et al., 2007). How- ever, its importance is likely to increase in the near future, in the light of a drastic increase of biogas production that is planned in the EU (Beurskens and Hekkenberg, 2011). Current anaerobic digestion facilities typically use slurry manure (i.e. less than 10% dry matter; Pain and Menzi (2011)) and due to slurry's low carbon (C) content and carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, addition of C-rich co-substrates is a usual practice (Hamelin et al., 2011). Grass and manure have been considered as key substrates
for co-digestion with manure in Estonia due to their high avail- ability and methane potential (Luna Del Risco et al., 2011).

This study focusses on the use of grass for manure-based anaerobic digestion. Two grass feedstocks are studied: reed ca- nary grass as one of the dedicated high-yielding energy grasses suggested for the Nordic climate (Kandel et al., 2013; Kukk et al., 2011; Moller et al., 2008) and grass from semi-natural grasslands as a currently clearly underused resource with considerable biogas potential (Melts et al., 2013). Semi-natural grasslands cover ca. 130,000 ha of land in Estonia (Kukk and Sammul, 2006) and are mostly located on floodplain meadows (Heinsoo et al., 2010). The areas are currently partly managed, i.e. the grass is generally cut once per year, but due to its low forage value it is still very often just left on the field. Thus Estonia, like many other countries, is seeking to find appropriate uses for the biomass from those areas (Melts et al., 2013). Continuing to extensively manage these semi-natural grasslands is necessary to maintain the high biodiversity value of those areas (Heinsoo et al., 2010). In this perspective, using the grass for bioenergy production could represent a sustainable op- portunity for supplying both renewable energy as well as essential ecosystem services (e.g. erosion regulation, soil carbon sequestra- tion). On the other hand, cultivated grasslands may be seen as a more reliable feedstock to supply a clean and uniform biomass stream to biogas plants. Various studies have highlighted high- yielding energy grasses as one of the most promising feedstock for biorefineries in Europe (Gerin et al., 2008; Korres et al., 2010; Smyth et al., 2009).

Using substrates for energy production takes them away from their initial use, and this change often has considerable environ- mental implications, whether positive or negative (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014; Pehme and Veromann, 2015; Styles et al., 2014; Tonini et al., 2016). Previous life cycle assessments of biogas production have studied the mono-digestion of manure (e.g. Cherubini al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2011; Lijó et al., 2014b), manure co-digestion with dedicated energy crops (Agostini et al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014; Lijó et al., 2014a; Pöschl et al., 2010; Styles et al., 2014; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014; Tonini et al., 2012), with by-products from agri- culture, food and feed industry (Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012; Fierro et al., 2014; Hamelin et al., 2014; Pöschl et al., 2010; Tonini et al., 2016) and with wastes/residues (Cimpan et al., 2015; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012; Fierro et al., 2014; Hamelin et al., 2014; Huopana et al., 2013; Pöschl et al., 2010; Styles et al., 2014; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). Those studies have highlighted the substantial environmental benefits of anaerobic digestion of manure instead of traditional manure management, and the need to focus on co-substrates not competing with food or feed crops for land use. Until now, studies investigating residual grass as a co-substrate for manure biogas focussed on roadside grass (e.g. De Vries et al., 2012) or grass from garden waste (e.g. Hamelin et al., 2014). Semi-natural grass has been included only in a few studies, but these were aimed more at developing a quantification model for land use change impacts (Tonini al., 2015) or greenhouse gas (GHG) emission factors for a variety of bioenergy pathways (Tonini et al., 2016).

In an endeavour to bridge this gap, the aim of the present study was to assess the environmental consequences of increased manure-based biogas production relying on grass as a key co- substrate. A consequential life cycle assessment (LCA) was per- formed to quantify the environmental impacts of three anaerobic digestion scenarios: i) mono-digestion of dairy cow manure; ii) manure co-digestion with cultivated energy grass (reed canary grass); and iii) manure co-digestion with residual grass from semi- natural grasslands (alluvial meadows).

Semi-natural grasslands cover ca. 130,000 ha of land in Estonia (Kukk and Sammul, 2006) and are mostly located on floodplain meadows (Heinsoo et al., 2010). The areas are currently partly managed, i.e. the grass is generally cut once per year, but due to its low forage value it is still very often just left on the field. Thus Estonia, like many other countries, is seeking to find appropriate uses for the biomass from those areas (Melts et al., 2013). Continuing to extensively manage these semi-natural grasslands is necessary to maintain the high biodiversity value of those areas (Heinsoo et al., 2010). In this perspective, using the grass for bioenergy production could represent a sustainable op- portunity for supplying both renewable energy as well as essential ecosystem services (e.g. erosion regulation, soil carbon sequestra- tion). On the other hand, cultivated grasslands may be seen as a more reliable feedstock to supply a clean and uniform biomass stream to biogas plants. Various studies have highlighted high- yielding energy grasses as one of the most promising feedstock for biorefineries in Europe (Gerin et al., 2008; Korres et al., 2010; Smyth et al., 2009).

Using substrates for energy production takes them away from their initial use, and this change often has considerable environ- mental implications, whether positive or negative (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014; Pehme and Veromann, 2015; Styles et al., 2014; Tonini et al., 2016). Previous life cycle assessments of biogas production have studied the mono-digestion of manure (e.g. Cherubini al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2011; Lijó et al., 2014b), manure co-digestion with dedicated energy crops (Agostini et al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014; Lijó et al., 2014a; Pöschl et al., 2010; Styles et al., 2014; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014; Tonini et al., 2012), with by-products from agri- culture, food and feed industry (Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012; Fierro et al., 2014; Hamelin et al., 2014; Pöschl et al., 2010; Tonini et al., 2016) and with wastes/residues (Cimpan et al., 2015; Croxatto Vega et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2012; Fierro et al., 2014; Hamelin et al., 2014; Huopana et al., 2013; Pöschl et al., 2010; Styles et al., 2014; Whiting and Azapagic, 2014). Those studies have highlighted the substantial environmental benefits of anaerobic digestion of manure instead of traditional manure management, and the need to focus on co-substrates not competing with food or feed crops for land use. Until now, studies investigating residual grass as a co-substrate for manure biogas focussed on roadside grass (e.g. De Vries et al., 2012) or grass from garden waste (e.g. Hamelin et al., 2014). Semi-natural grass has been included only in a few studies, but these were aimed more at developing a quantification model for land use change impacts (Tonini al., 2015) or greenhouse gas (GHG) emission factors for a variety of bioenergy pathways (Tonini et al., 2016).

In an endeavour to bridge this gap, the aim of the present study was to assess the environmental consequences of increased manure-based biogas production relying on grass as a key co- substrate. A consequential life cycle assessment (LCA) was per- formed to quantify the environmental impacts of three anaerobic digestion scenarios: i) mono-digestion of dairy cow manure; ii) manure co-digestion with cultivated energy grass (reed canary grass); and iii) manure co-digestion with residual grass from semi- natural grasslands (alluvial meadows).
8: This study was performed through consequential LCA (Finnveden et al., 2009) as the most suitable approach to support decision making processes (Weidema, 2003). The functional unit (FU) to which all input and output flows were normalized was the management of one (wet) tonne (t) of dairy cow manure ex-animal. The prefix "ex" is, throughout this manuscript, referring to the composition of each substrate immediately after leaving the stage following the prefix. Manure ex-animal thus refers to the manure as freshly excreted by the animals. The geographical scope of the study was Estonia (Nordic conditions); foreground data (e.g. biomass yield, fertilizer application, technologies and regulations) were thus based the Estonian context. Impacts associated with capital goods in the foreground processes were excluded due to lack of data. Background data (e.g. imported fertilizers, electricity) were included in the model by combining the Ecoinvent v.3.2 consequential database (Weidema et al., 2013) and relevant sci- entific literature. The impact assessment methods used for this study were the EDIP2003 methodology (Hauschild and Potting, 2005) for the acidification and eutrophication impacts (dis- tinguishing for nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) as the limiting nutrient) and the IPCC 2013 for assessing the global warming po- tential (GWP, 100 years horizon time; (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, 2013)). These impact categories relate especially with the carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus flows, and are thus seen as the most relevant for the agricultural systems studied herein. The assessment was facilitated by the LCA software SimaPro 8.2.0.

10: Three different biogas scenarios were considered in this study: mono-digestion of dairy cow manure, manure co-digestion with reed canary grass and co-digestion with grass from semi-natural areas. All processes affected by the demand for manure-based biogas were included in the LCA model, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (example for the reed canary grass scenario). Consequential LCA is based on market information to identify which activities are affected by a change. In this study each output of the system (including co-products) is assumed to substitute marginal products (full elasticity of supply i.e. 1:1 substitution approach). The pro- cesses and technologies to include in a consequential life cycle study are the processes and technologies actually affected by the studied product substitution (Weidema, 2003). In a long term perspective it can be assumed that fossil resources will be phased out by renewables in order to achieve the political CO2 reduction targets. In this perspective the energy sources with the highest emissions are expected to be the fuels reacting to increased elec- tricity production from waste and biomass (Tonini et al., 2013).

Each biogas scenario was assumed to avoid the conventional management of dairy cow manure (i.e. reference scenario), which represents the current situation (i.e. counterfactual) where manure does not undergo any treatment process. Reference manure man- agement has three main stages: slurry is stored in-house, then pumped to an outdoor concrete slurry tank (natural crust cover) from which it is finally applied to fields when suitable. Further details on the processes and emission flows considered for the reference manure management in Estonia are available in Hamelin et al. (2013). The composition of substrates, emission data, mass balances and process flows for all biogas scenarios are described in the Supplementary material.
In each biogas scenario, manure is collected from the in-house storage (manure ex-housing) and transferred to the anaerobic

Fig. 1. Key processes and flows considered in the system boundary for the reed canary grass co-digestion scenario, per functional unit (1 t of manure ex-animal). Dotted lines indicate the avoided processes.

digester. The dry matter (DM) content of dairy manure prior to its input to the digester (i.e. manure ex-housing) was taken to 11% and volatile solids (VS) were considered to represent 82% of the DM (Hamelin et al., 2013). A 2-steps mesophilic digestion process was considered, with the resulting biogas consisting of 65% CH4 and 35% CO2, with a density of 1.158 kg Nm and a lower heating value (LHV) of 22.88 MJ Nm (Hamelin et al., 2014). Fugitive CH4 losses from the anaerobic digestion process were taken as 1% of the overall CH4 produced, assuming the implementation of state-of- the-art biogas technologies (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). The biogas was assumed to be used for combined heat and power production (CHP), considering a biogas engine with 46% efficiency for heat and 40% efficiency for electricity (Hamelin et al., 2011). The produced heat was assumed to be used in the district heating grid. Based on Poeschl et al. (2012), 60% of the net heat produced was assumed to substitute heat from a natural gas boiler (marginal heat), the rest being lost for a variety of reasons (e.g. variation in seasonal demand, etc.). Natural gas is considered as the marginal heat for several European countries (Tonini et al., 2013). The produced electricity was assumed to be put on the grid, thus replacing fossil electricity which is here represented by coal elec- tricity. Though oil shale electricity is the main source of electricity and considered as the short-term marginal electricity for Estonia (Moora and Lahtvee, 2009), there is no appropriate LCA dataset available for this type of electricity, hence coal electricity being used as a proxy. For the co-substrate scenarios, it was assumed, based on Hamelin et al. (2011), that co-substrates were added to manure to get a biomass mixture with 10% DM after the first digestion step. Similarly to non-digested (conventional) manure, the digestate was considered to be stored in a concrete tank and applied on field after its storage, displacing mineral nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium. Marginal fertilizers considered were calcium ammonium nitrate, diammonium phosphate and potas- sium chloride (Hamelin et al., 2014), and the marginal use of arable

land was considered to be for barley cultivation (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). Usually the crop that will be affected in Europe is barley, since this is the crop with the lowest gross margin (Weidema, 2003). The key processes considered and flows for the cultivated RCG scenario are presented in Fig. 1. The specific system boundary issues for this scenario are further explained in the scenario section, while the process flows for all other scenarios are illustrated in the Supplementary material.

Biogenic carbon flows were accounted for whenever relevant. This included essentially the carbon dioxide (CO2) uptake by plants, the methane (CH4) and CO2 emissions along the biogas production, combustion and manure management chain (digestate applica- tion), as well as soil carbon changes. The latter was estimated based on the approach of Petersen et al. (2013), considering that 10% of the carbon added to the soil is permanently sequestrated in a 100 years perspective. The overall accounting of biogenic carbon flows is illustrated in Fig. 2.

Land use changes caused by the reed canary grass production displacing the marginal use of arable land (cultivation of barley) were included as described in section 3.3 and in the Supplementary material. All the key system boundary issues considered in the LCA model are summarized in Table 1.

Similarly to non-digested (conventional) manure, the digestate was considered to be stored in a concrete tank and applied on field after its storage, displacing mineral nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium. Marginal fertilizers considered were calcium ammonium nitrate, diammonium phosphate and potas- sium chloride (Hamelin et al., 2014), and the marginal use of arable

land was considered to be for barley cultivation (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). Usually the crop that will be affected in Europe is barley, since this is the crop with the lowest gross margin (Weidema, 2003). The key processes considered and flows for the cultivated RCG scenario are presented in Fig. 1. The specific system boundary issues for this scenario are further explained in the scenario section, while the process flows for all other scenarios are illustrated in the Supplementary material.

Biogenic carbon flows were accounted for whenever relevant. This included essentially the carbon dioxide (CO2) uptake by plants, the methane (CH4) and CO2 emissions along the biogas production, combustion and manure management chain (digestate applica- tion), as well as soil carbon changes. The latter was estimated based on the approach of Petersen et al. (2013), considering that 10% of the carbon added to the soil is permanently sequestrated in a 100 years perspective. The overall accounting of biogenic carbon flows is illustrated in Fig. 2.

Land use changes caused by the reed canary grass production displacing the marginal use of arable land (cultivation of barley) were included as described in section 3.3 and in the Supplementary material. All the key system boundary issues considered in the LCA model are summarized in Table 1.
15: Three different biogas scenarios were considered in this study: mono-digestion of dairy cow manure, manure co-digestion with reed canary grass and co-digestion with grass from semi-natural areas. All processes affected by the demand for manure-based biogas were included in the LCA model, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (example for the reed canary grass scenario). Consequential LCA is based on market information to identify which activities are affected by a change. In this study each output of the system (including co-products) is assumed to substitute marginal products (full elasticity of supply i.e. 1:1 substitution approach). The pro- cesses and technologies to include in a consequential life cycle study are the processes and technologies actually affected by the studied product substitution (Weidema, 2003). In a long term perspective it can be assumed that fossil resources will be phased out by renewables in order to achieve the political CO2 reduction targets. In this perspective the energy sources with the highest emissions are expected to be the fuels reacting to increased elec- tricity production from waste and biomass (Tonini et al., 2013).

Each biogas scenario was assumed to avoid the conventional management of dairy cow manure (i.e. reference scenario), which represents the current situation (i.e. counterfactual) where manure does not undergo any treatment process. Reference manure man- agement has three main stages: slurry is stored in-house, then pumped to an outdoor concrete slurry tank (natural crust cover) from which it is finally applied to fields when suitable. Further details on the processes and emission flows considered for the reference manure management in Estonia are available in Hamelin et al. (2013). The composition of substrates, emission data, mass balances and process flows for all biogas scenarios are described in the Supplementary material.
In each biogas scenario, manure is collected from the in-house storage (manure ex-housing) and transferred to the anaerobic

digester. The dry matter (DM) content of dairy manure prior to its input to the digester (i.e. manure ex-housing) was taken to 11% and volatile solids (VS) were considered to represent 82% of the DM (Hamelin et al., 2013). A 2-steps mesophilic digestion process was considered, with the resulting biogas consisting of 65% CH4 and 35% CO2, with a density of 1.158 kg Nm and a lower heating value (LHV) of 22.88 MJ Nm (Hamelin et al., 2014). The produced heat was assumed to be used in the district heating grid. Based on Poeschl et al. (2012), 60% of the net heat produced was assumed to substitute heat from a natural gas boiler (marginal heat), the rest being lost for a variety of reasons (e.g. variation in seasonal demand, etc.). Natural gas is considered as the marginal heat for several European countries (Tonini et al., 2013). The produced electricity was assumed to be put on the grid, thus replacing fossil electricity which is here represented by coal elec- tricity. 18: <header>3.2. Scenario 2 (S2): manure co-digestion with natural grass </header>

In scenario 2, dairy cow slurry is co-digested with natural grass (NG) silage. NG is collected once per year from semi-natural alluvial meadows where neither agricultural inputs are used nor soil cultivation is practiced. The grass yield is considered to be 5.52 t of DM ha-1-superscript(1) according to Melts et al. (2013), which translates to a C uptake of 2594 kg y-1, considering a C content of 0.47 kg C kg-1 DM in the grass above-ground biomass (IPCC, 2006). The grass is har- vested, transported to a biogas plant and stored as silage; a trans- port distance of 25 km has been assumed. Currently, most of the grass from natural grasslands is left unharvested or is harvested, but still left on the field (i.e. not utilized in any way), hence diverting it to biogas production avoids the CO2 emissions that would have occurred from its natural decomposition on-site (counterfactual). Avoided grass decay was modelled considering that all the grass-C not ending up in the permanent soil C pool would have been oxidized and emitted as CO2 in the atmosphere.
NG silage, when entering the anaerobic digester, is considered to have a BMP of 269 Nm3 t-¹ VS (Melts et al., 2013).
3.3. Scenario 3 (S3): manure co-digestion with reed canary grass

This scenario is based on co-digestion of dairy cow slurry and reed canary grass (RCG) silage (Fig. 1). In this system, the grass is produced specifically for biogas; it is fertilized and harvested twice per year. RCG production data were based on Noormets et al., 2007, including an average grass yield of 8.23 t DM ha 1 (16 years plantation, 2 cuts per year). The average carbon uptake of RCG aboveground harvested biomass was considered 3.9 t C per year and the amount of CO2 sequestrated to soil was assumed to be 10% of the input i.e. 1.45 tonne of CO2 ha-¹ y-1 on average. The grass is harvested, transported to a biogas plant and stored as silage. As in scenario 2, a transport distance of 25 km was considered. A BMP of 296 Nm³ t- 1 VS was considered for RCG (Seppälä et al., 2009). RCG production is considered to replace the use of land for barley

cultivation (counterfactual). This barley thus has to be produced somewhere else, whether through intensification or expansion of arable land, a reaction referred to as "indirect land use changes" (ILUC). The quantification of GHG emissions due to ILUC is often pointed out as problematic because of the uncertainty it involves, and because those changes are not directly observable (Ahlgren and Di Lucia, 2014). As a result, different approaches and emission factors have been proposed and used to this end (De Rosa et al., 2015). Based on the review performed by Broch et al. (2013), the range of net ILUC emissions were found to be from 130 to 350 CO2 eq. ha demanded. Considering the large variability of ILUC fig- ures, this study includes the minimum and maximum ILUC values presented by Broch et al. (2013), which corresponds to 6.5 t of CO2 eq. (used as a baseline for this scenario) and up to 17.5 t of CO2 eq. ha (considered for the sensitivity analysis), assuming a 20 years amortization. The details of all scenarios are described in Supplementary material.

26: In the perspective of reducing GHG emissions, the LCA results indicate that manure is better used for biogas than managed conventionally, as reflected by the overall negative result (i.e. GHG savings) for the mono-digestion scenario (S1; Fig. 3). This is mainly caused by avoided raw manure handling (mostly due to CH4 emissions from outdoor storage, which are instead captured and emitted as CO2 in the biogas scenarios) and by avoided fossil electricity production. Though there are no strong differences be- tween the performance of S1 (mono-digestion) and S3 (co-diges- tion with RCG), Scenario 2 (co-digestion with natural grass) outperformed the other scenarios for the global warming impact, because of the lower induced impacts (i.e. no cultivation needed) compared to dedicated grass (S2 reduced GWP 41% compared to S1 and 35% compared to S3).

As reflected by the increase of the net result observed for the sensitivity scenario S4, indirect land use changes, when involved, can change the overall conclusion regarding the performance of a bioenergy scenario, depending on its magnitude. In the case of the RCG scenario, however, even a high ILUC brings the net perfor- mance just slightly above zero. This means that digesting manure and cultivating RCG grass (instead of barley) is more or less equivalent, in terms of GHG balance, to managing the manure conventionally and using the land for barley cultivation.

Higher fugitive methane losses from the biogas plant (4% CH4 lost), longer transport distance (250 km) or lower yield of the natural grass (2 DM ha- instead of 5.5 t DM ha slightly decreased the global warming performance of the NG scenario (between 7% and 16%). In the sensitivity case where wind electricity is avoided instead of fossil (coal) electricity (S6 in Fig. 3), the net GWP impact becomes slightly under zero. This highlights that even in a renewable electricity future where displacing electricity gives no GWP improvement, using manure for the production of biogas along with RCG grass does allow GWP benefits as compared to the counterfactual.

Most of the induced greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions for all scenarios originated from field application of the digestate, where a large share of the non-degraded carbon is emitted as CO2. Other induced GHG emissions include the storage of the digestate (mostly because of nitrous oxide), the biogas combustion when producing CHP (oxidation of the C as CO2 and CH4 losses at the engine) (CHP) and as a result of indirect land use change (CO2) in the specific case of scenarios 3 and 4 (Fig. 3).

highest acidification impact (4 times higher compared to mono- digestion and 2 times higher than NG), among others because of the additional emissions from the grass production process. It also reflects that RCG has a lower dry matter content than NG, and thus more of it could be included in the manure-grass mixture (Table 3). 3: The LCA results show that digesting cow manure does not have a clear effect on the aquatic eutrophication impact, as reflected by the near-zero impact of the mono-digestion scenario, whether for N- eutrophication or P-eutrophication (Fig. 3). Yet, similarly to the acidification impact, the more there are nutrients (nitrogen and phosphorus) involved in the system due to the addition of co- substrates, the greater the impact is. The main induced impacts for N- and P-eutrophication were caused by the digestate man- agement and by the extra emissions related to fertilization in the RCG case. All scenarios led to a net increased impact for N-eutro- phication, and reduced impact for P-eutrophication, where the credit for avoided fossil electricity was more important. Compared to mono-digestion, N-eutrophication was 2-3 times higher for the NG scenario and 10 times higher for RCG scenario, while P-eutro- phication was 1.3-2 times lower for all the co-digestion scenarios (except for S6; wind electricity). The latter exception reflects the P flows involved with coal-based electricity (and not with wind- electricity).
4: Generally, mono-digestion of manure does demonstrate a clear potential for improving the environmental performance of manure management, showing net impacts either reduced or close to neutral for most of the impact categories, as compared to conven- tional manure management. This conclusion is in line with other studies (e.g. Agostini et al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). Still, the energy output of manure mono-digestion is low compared to what is obtained with co-digestion (up to 63% lower in the present study; Table 3), thus this might not be economically viable (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003; Hamelin et al., 2011).

The natural grass scenario showed the greatest net GWP per- formance, which is essentially a consequence of using this grass for energy, instead of leaving it on-field. Using grass residues for manure co-digestion has shown promising impact reduction po- tentials also in previous studies (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014; Tonini et al., 2016). During the last century, semi-natural grasslands have become an underused resource due to the devel- opment of more intensive livestock systems with higher re- quirements to grass yield and quality (Isselstein et al., 2005). Continuing extensive management of semi-natural grasslands would help to maintain their high biodiversity value - an impor- tant environmental aspect that is often not included in LCA mainly because of the lack of a methodological consensus (Curran et al., 2016; Holma et al., 2013; Teixeira et al., 2016). If the counterfac- tual use of natural grass was different, for example if it was used as

animal forage, then using it for co-digestion would most likely show no environmental benefits due to the need to substitute the feed, as also highlighted in previous studies (e.g. De Vries et al., 2012; Tonini et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2014).

The grass yield from Estonian floodplain meadows is estimated to be 113 kilotonnes (kt) of DM (Heinsoo et al., 2010). If half of this currently unused biomass would be harvested and used for anaerobic co-digestion with manure as presented in this study, it would enable to digest 490 kt of manure ex-animal, and the amount of produced biogas would be 3.79 X 107 Nm3 energy It would allow to surpass the current target of 0.5 PJ of energy produced from biogas set in the National Renewable Energy Action Plan for 2020 (Roose et al., 2012). However, there are still several practical aspects which need further investigation, including technological questions such as how to harvest the grass under seasonal flooding, as well as questions related to the eco- nomic feasibility of the whole natural grass to biogas supply chain.

The RCG system, on the other hand, presents much simpler lo- gistics and likely allows for a better business plan, but as high- lighted in the sensitivity analysis results (S4; Fig. 3), its GWP performance is strongly dependent upon the ILUC factor selected for modelling. Currently, there are different approaches with sig- nificant variation proposed to estimate the CO2 emissions due to ILUC, and the level of uncertainty of all models is very high (De Rosa et al., 2015; Schmidt et al., 2015). Albeit uncertain, the GHG emis- sions due to ILUC must nevertheless be accounted for, because they are not zero, and because of the implication they have for policy making. In the present case, even a high ILUC factor brings the environmental performance close to neutrality (though slightly above). However, it should be highlighted that the ILUC factors used herein (baseline and sensitivity) are relatively higher than those found in recent studies (e.g. 4.1 t CO2 eq. per generic hectare demanded per y in Tonini et al., 2016). Therefore, the use of Esto- nian arable land for high-yielding grass such as RCG in order to produce biogas appears as a solution that does lead to an improvement of the global warming impact, although this does not apply for the N-related impacts (acidification, N-eutrophication). On this basis, additional research endeavouring to minimize the technological and non-technological barriers related to the bio- energy use of unharvested semi-natural grasslands appears desirable.

A significant share of global warming reduction for all scenarios was caused by the avoided fossil electricity (here represented by coal electricity), and this was significantly reduced when wind was the marginal electricity source, as also highlighted previously (e.g. Tonini et al., 2016). In such future, however, biogas may not be used for CHP as considered here but upgraded and stored in the natural gas grid, where it can be used as a flexible electricity source when e.g. the wind does not blow. In such case, the avoided marginal flexible power may likely be a bio-based one, whose avoidance would also involve significant benefits (Wenzel et al., 2014). On this basis, it is expected that the overall negative GWP obtained in the LCA results (Fig. 3) would also hold in the future. The current Estonian electricity production is mainly based on oil-shale, whose GHG emissions per kwh of electricity are estimated to be 1.3 times higher than those of coal-based electricity (used in this study), based on the Environmental Product Declaration data of the elec- tricity company Eesti Energia (2008). Hence, the actual GHG sav- ings from biogas based energy replacing oil-shale electricity would be most likely even more promising.

Though fugitive losses should be minimized also from a pro- ducer perspective, losses of 1% of the methane produced are likely underestimating the current situation, as shown by a recent study where average losses of 4% have been measured for Danish biogas plants. These leakages, however, can be kept under 1% through
better maintenance and relatively low-cost repairs (AgroTech A/S, 2015).

The transport distance of co-substrates has shown a rather low contribution to the net environmental impact of biogas production, as also observed in other studies (Dressler et al., 2012; Lijó et al., 2014a; Patterson et al., 2011). Similarly, a lower grass yield lead to only slightly higher impacts due to the increased grass harvesting and transportation operations as the grass has to be collected from a larger area to get the same amount of DM input per FU.

The various sensitivity analyses performed for the NG scenario (S5-S8) highlighted that even under a mix of unfavourable condi- tions, i.e. low grass yield, high fugitive CH4 losses and long transport distances aggregated in one scenario, the net GWP result would be still be below zero (-141 kg CO2-eq). This also applies for P- eutrophication.

On this basis, additional research endeavouring to minimize the technological and non-technological barriers related to the bio- energy use of unharvested semi-natural grasslands appears desirable.

A significant share of global warming reduction for all scenarios was caused by the avoided fossil electricity (here represented by coal electricity), and this was significantly reduced when wind was the marginal electricity source, as also highlighted previously (e.g. Tonini et al., 2016). In such future, however, biogas may not be used for CHP as considered here but upgraded and stored in the natural gas grid, where it can be used as a flexible electricity source when e.g. the wind does not blow. In such case, the avoided marginal flexible power may likely be a bio-based one, whose avoidance would also involve significant benefits (Wenzel et al., 2014). On this basis, it is expected that the overall negative GWP obtained in the LCA results (Fig. 3) would also hold in the future. The current Estonian electricity production is mainly based on oil-shale, whose GHG emissions per kwh of electricity are estimated to be 1.3 times higher than those of coal-based electricity (used in this study), based on the Environmental Product Declaration data of the elec- tricity company Eesti Energia (2008). Hence, the actual GHG sav- ings from biogas based energy replacing oil-shale electricity would be most likely even more promising.

Though fugitive losses should be minimized also from a pro- ducer perspective, losses of 1% of the methane produced are likely underestimating the current situation, as shown by a recent study where average losses of 4% have been measured for Danish biogas plants. These leakages, however, can be kept under 1% through
better maintenance and relatively low-cost repairs (AgroTech A/S, 2015).

The transport distance of co-substrates has shown a rather low contribution to the net environmental impact of biogas production, as also observed in other studies (Dressler et al., 2012; Lijó et al., 2014a; Patterson et al., 2011). Similarly, a lower grass yield lead to only slightly higher impacts due to the increased grass harvesting and transportation operations as the grass has to be collected from a larger area to get the same amount of DM input per FU.

The various sensitivity analyses performed for the NG scenario (S5-S8) highlighted that even under a mix of unfavourable condi- tions, i.e. low grass yield, high fugitive CH4 losses and long transport distances aggregated in one scenario, the net GWP result would be still be below zero (-141 kg CO2-eq). This also applies for P- eutrophication.
9: Generally, mono-digestion of manure does demonstrate a clear potential for improving the environmental performance of manure management, showing net impacts either reduced or close to neutral for most of the impact categories, as compared to conven- tional manure management. This conclusion is in line with other studies (e.g. Agostini et al., 2015; De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014). Still, the energy output of manure mono-digestion is low compared to what is obtained with co-digestion (up to 63% lower in the present study; Table 3), thus this might not be economically viable (Angelidaki and Ellegaard, 2003; Hamelin et al., 2011).

The natural grass scenario showed the greatest net GWP per- formance, which is essentially a consequence of using this grass for energy, instead of leaving it on-field. Using grass residues for manure co-digestion has shown promising impact reduction po- tentials also in previous studies (De Vries et al., 2012; Hamelin et al., 2014; Tonini et al., 2016). During the last century, semi-natural grasslands have become an underused resource due to the devel- opment of more intensive livestock systems with higher re- quirements to grass yield and quality (Isselstein et al., 2005). Continuing extensive management of semi-natural grasslands would help to maintain their high biodiversity value - an impor- tant environmental aspect that is often not included in LCA mainly because of the lack of a methodological consensus (Curran et al., 2016; Holma et al., 2013; Teixeira et al., 2016). If the counterfac- tual use of natural grass was different, for example if it was used as

animal forage, then using it for co-digestion would most likely show no environmental benefits due to the need to substitute the feed, as also highlighted in previous studies (e.g. De Vries et al., 2012; Tonini et al., 2016; Styles et al., 2014).

The grass yield from Estonian floodplain meadows is estimated to be 113 kilotonnes (kt) of DM (Heinsoo et al., 2010). If half of this currently unused biomass would be harvested and used for anaerobic co-digestion with manure as presented in this study, it would enable to digest 490 kt of manure ex-animal, and the amount of produced biogas would be 3.79 X 107 Nm3 energy It would allow to surpass the current target of 0.5 PJ of energy produced from biogas set in the National Renewable Energy Action Plan for 2020 (Roose et al., 2012). However, there are still several practical aspects which need further investigation, including technological questions such as how to harvest the grass under seasonal flooding, as well as questions related to the eco- nomic feasibility of the whole natural grass to biogas supply chain.

The RCG system, on the other hand, presents much simpler lo- gistics and likely allows for a better business plan, but as high- lighted in the sensitivity analysis results (S4; Fig. 3), its GWP performance is strongly dependent upon the ILUC factor selected for modelling. Currently, there are different approaches with sig- nificant variation proposed to estimate the CO2 emissions due to ILUC, and the level of uncertainty of all models is very high (De Rosa et al., 2015; Schmidt et al., 2015). Albeit uncertain, the GHG emis- sions due to ILUC must nevertheless be accounted for, because they are not zero, and because of the implication they have for policy making. In the present case, even a high ILUC factor brings the environmental performance close to neutrality (though slightly above). However, it should be highlighted that the ILUC factors used herein (baseline and sensitivity) are relatively higher than those found in recent studies (e.g. 4.1 t CO2 eq. per generic hectare demanded per y in Tonini et al., 2016). 10: Manure-based biogas production has significant environmental benefits, and grass is seen as an important underused resource for manure co-digestion in Estonia. This study showed that the use of both cultivated (reed canary grass) and semi-natural grass for the production of manure-based biogas yields to an improvement of the overall environmental performance of the system for global warming and P-eutrophication impacts, in comparison to the counterfactual where manure is managed conventionally, natural grass is not harvested and barley is cultivated instead of reed canary grass. This conclusion, however, does not apply for N-related im- pacts such as acidification and N-eutrophication, albeit these im- pacts could be minimized through the application of relevant mitigation technologies. Considering that grass co-digestion is still not widespread throughout Europe, and that alternative uses of semi-natural grassland are looked for, this study is timely in vali- dating the environmental performance of grass use for biogas. The conclusions of this study, however, are very much dependent upon the counterfactual use of the grass substrate considered, and may not apply if e.g. the natural grass would have instead been used for animal feed.
